medical_history.chief_complaint: inexplicable progressive weight gain, muscle tremor, general fatigue, perspiration, hypercholesterolemia, exophthalmos, an enlargement of the thyroid gland, and typical cushingoid features like abdominal striae, 'moon face', central obesity, and a 'buffalo hump'
medical_history.history_of_present_illness: Presented in September 2003 at the age of 41 years with symptoms. Laboratory testing showed increased 24 h urinary free cortisol, significant elevation of basal serum cortisol and ACTH, and hyperthyroidism. Ectopic ACTH syndrome was suspected. Subtotal thyroidectomy and neurosurgical removal of a small lesion of the neurohypophysis did not lead to remission of hypercortisolism. Treatment was started with metyrapone combined with hydrocortisone replacement.
medical_history.past_medical_history: Hypercortisolism, diabetes mellitus, hypertension, hypercholesterolemia, multinodular goiter

physical_examination.general_examination: Obesity (110 kg bodyweight, BMI 34.7 kg/m²), pulse of 70 bpm, no signs of cardiopulmonary decompensation but cushingoid features like 'moon face', plethora, 'buffalo hump' and central obesity

laboratory_examinations.procedure_name: Overnight suppression test with 2 mg dexamethasone

laboratory_examinations.findings: Serum cortisol decreased by 38% of basal values

laboratory_examinations.procedure_name: CRH stimulated serum cortisol and plasma ACTH

laboratory_examinations.findings: 834 nmol/L at 8 a.m., 806 nmol/L at 1 p.m., 765 nmol/L at 4 p.m., 749 at 8 p.m. and 453 at midnight (normal range: 8 – 10 a.m.: 187 – 724 nmol/L, 4 – 6 p.m.: 104 – 397 nmol/L)
No suppression of morning cortisol (690 nmol/L at 8, 549 nmol/L at 11 p.m. and 546 nmol/L at 8 a.m. next day)
Blood glucose level of 15 mmol/L after 2 h

radiographic_examinations.findings: Focal octreotide enhancement in the abdomen interpreted to be nonspecific

radiographic_examinations.procedure_name: CT scans of thorax and abdomen
radiographic_examinations.findings: Negative except for the left-sided adrenal tumor
Tracer accumulation in at least 3 spots in projection of the upper and middle part of the right abdomen
Increased glucose metabolism of the abdominal area
21 × 15 mm tumor mass in the right abdomen
Tracer enhancement within the right lower abdomen

radiographic_examinations.procedure_name: MRI and CT scans of the abdomen
radiographic_examinations.findings: Could not clearly identify any tumors

radiographic_examinations.procedure_name: Fusion imaging of MRI and PET scans
radiographic_examinations.findings: Significant enhancement within the right lower abdomen in correlation with localized mural thickening of the ileum

other_tests.findings: Could only partly visualize the ileum due to massive loops of the sigmoid colon
Small tumor confined to the terminal ileum and multiple enlarged lymph nodes in the adjacent mesentery

diagnosis.final_diagnosis: Ectopic Cushing’s Syndrome caused by a well-differentiated ACTH-secreting neuroendocrine carcinoma of the ileum
diagnosis.diagnostic_reasoning: The diagnosis was based on laboratory testing showing hypercortisolism and elevated ACTH levels, along with imaging studies (Ga-68 DOTATOC-PET scan) that identified the primary tumor in the ileum. The patient's symptoms included weight gain, muscle tremor, general fatigue, and cushingoid features. The ectopic ACTH syndrome was confirmed through various tests, including dexamethasone suppression tests and inferior petrosal sinus sampling (IPSS), which did not show significant central-to-peripheral ACTH gradients.
